17	
18	\section{Setup and motivation}
19	
20	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
21	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
22	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
23	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
24	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
25	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
26	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
27	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
28	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
29	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
30	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
31	
32	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
33	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
34	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
35	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
36	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
37	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
38	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
39	\[
40	  \begin{array}{ccc}
41	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
42	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
43	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
44	  \end{array}
45	\]
46	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
47	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
48	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
49	
50	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
51	
52	\begin{definition}
53	
54	\leanok
55	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
56	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
58	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
59	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
60	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
61	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
62	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
63	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
64	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
65	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
66	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
67	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
68	  % the unit ideal, see
69	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
70	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
71	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
72	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
73	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
74	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
75	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
76	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
77	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
78	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
79	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
80	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
81	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
82	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
83	  \[
84	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
85	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
86	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
87	  \]
88	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
89	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
90	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
91	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
92	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
93	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
94	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
95	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
96	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
97	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
98	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
99	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
100	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
101	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
102	\end{definition}
103	
104	\begin{definition}
105	
106	\leanok
107	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
108	  \label{def:cech_complex}
109	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
110	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
111	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
112	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
113	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
114	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
115	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
116	  % is identified with the complex
117	  % $$
118	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
119	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
120	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
121	  % \ldots
122	  % $$
123	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
124	  % \begin{equation}
125	  % \label{equation-extended}
126	  % 0 \to
127	  % M \to
128	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
129	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
130	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
131	  % \ldots
132	  % \end{equation}
133	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
134	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
135	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
136	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
137	  vanishing).}
138	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
139	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
140	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
141	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
142	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
143	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
144	  in degree \(p\) it is
145	  \[
146	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
147	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
148	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
149	  \]
150	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
151	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
152	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
153	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
154	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
155	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
156	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
157	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
158	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
159	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
160	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
161	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
162	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
163	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
164	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
165	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
166	\end{definition}
167	
168	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
169	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
170	
171	It is convenient to factor the construction of
172	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
173	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
174	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
175	
176	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
177	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
178	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
179	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
180	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
181	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
182	\[
183	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
184	    \;=\;
185	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
186	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
187	\]
188	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
189	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
190	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
191	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
192	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
193	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
194	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
195	
196	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
197	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
198	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
199	\[
200	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
201	  \qquad
202	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
203	\]
204	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
205	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
206	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
207	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
208	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
209	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
210	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
211	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
212	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
213	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
214	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
215	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
216	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
217	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
218	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
219	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
220	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
221	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
222	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
223	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
224	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
225	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
226	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
227	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
228	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
229	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
230	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
231	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
232	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
233	
234	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
235	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
236	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
237	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
238	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
239	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
240	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
241	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
242	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
243	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
244	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
245	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
246	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
247	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
248	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
249	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
250	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
251	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
252	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
253	
254	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
255	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
256	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
257	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
258	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Arrow of an affine cover]
262	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
264	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
265	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
266	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
267	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
268	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
269	  apply.
270	\end{definition}
271	
272	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
273	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
275	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
276	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
277	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
278	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
279	  \[
280	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
281	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
282	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
283	  \]
284	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
285	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
286	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
287	\end{definition}
288	
289	\begin{definition}\leanok
290	[Push--pull object map]
291	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
293	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
294	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
295	  \[
296	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
297	  \]
298	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
299	\end{definition}
300	
301	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
302	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
304	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
305	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
306	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
307	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
308	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
309	  \[
310	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
312	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
314	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \;=\;
316	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
318	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
319	  \]
320	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
321	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
322	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
323	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
324	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
325	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
330	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
332	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
333	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
334	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
335	\end{lemma}
336	
337	\begin{proof}
338	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
339	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
340	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
341	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
342	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
343	\end{proof}
344	
345	\begin{lemma}
346	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
347	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
349	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
350	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
351	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
352	  \[
353	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
354	  \]
355	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
356	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
357	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
358	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
359	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
364	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
365	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
366	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
367	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
368	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
369	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
370	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
371	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
372	\end{proof}
373	
374	\begin{lemma}
375	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
376	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
377	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
378	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
379	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
380	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
381	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
382	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
383	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
384	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
385	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
386	  \[
387	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
388	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
389	    \;=\;
390	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
391	  \]
392	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
393	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
394	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
395	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
400	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
401	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
402	\end{proof}
403	
404	\begin{lemma}
405	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
406	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
408	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
409	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
410	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
411	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
412	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
413	  collapses to the transport-light form
414	  \[
415	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
416	      \;\circ\; e_2
417	    \;=\;
418	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
419	      \;\circ\; e_3,
420	  \]
421	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
422	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
423	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
424	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
425	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
426	  composition (pentagon) law.
427	\end{lemma}
428	
429	\begin{proof}
430	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
431	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
432	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
433	\end{proof}

435	\begin{definition}
436	[Push--pull functor]
437	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
438	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
439	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
440	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
441	  \[
442	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
443	  \]

445	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
446	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
447	  laws established above: the identity law
448	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
449	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
450	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
451	\end{definition}
452	
453	\begin{definition}
454	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
455	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
456	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
457	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
458	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
459	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
460	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
461	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
462	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
463	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
464	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
465	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
466	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
467	\end{definition}
468	
469	\begin{definition}\leanok
470	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
471	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
473	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
474	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
475	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
476	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
477	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
478	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
479	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
480	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
481	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
482	\end{definition}

484	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
485	
486	\begin{definition}
487	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
488	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
490	  \mathlibok
491	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
492	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
493	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
494	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
495	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
496	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
497	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
498	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
499	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
500	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
501	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
502	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
503	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
504	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
505	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
506	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
507	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
508	\end{definition}
509	
510	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
511	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
512	
513	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
514	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
515	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
516	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
517	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
518	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
519	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
520	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
521	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
522	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
523	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
524	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
525	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
526	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
527	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
528	
529	\begin{definition}
530	
531	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
532	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
534	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
535	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
536	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
537	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
538	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
539	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
540	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
541	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
542	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
543	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
544	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
545	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
546	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
547	  % as an $R_f$-module.
548	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
549	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
550	  % are the maps
551	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
552	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
553	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
554	  items (4)--(5).}
555	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
556	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
557	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
558	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
559	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
560	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
561	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
562	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
563	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
564	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
565	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
566	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
567	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
568	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
569	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
570	
571	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
572	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
573	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
574	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
575	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
576	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
577	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
578	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
579	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
580	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
581	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
582	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
583	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
584	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
585	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
586	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
587	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
588	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
589	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
590	
591	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
592	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
593	  intersection identity
594	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
595	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
596	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
597	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
598	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
599	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
600	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
601	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
602	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
603	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
604	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
605	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
606	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
607	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
608	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
609	
610	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
611	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
612	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
613	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
614	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
615	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
616	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
617	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
618	  restriction map through these comparisons.
619	\end{definition}

621	\begin{lemma}
622	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
623	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
624	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
625	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
626	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
627	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
628	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,

630	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
631	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
632	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
633	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
634	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,
635	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_unique,
636	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff,
637	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff_transport_eq_comparison,
638	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoface,
639	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechLocalized_exact,
640	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechPrepend,
641	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hcomm,
642	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hsh,
643	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hu,
644	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod,
645	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_cons,
646	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_succAbove_dvd}
647	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact,
648	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
649	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
650	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
651	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
652	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
653	  % L58--67).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
655	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
656	  % is identified with the complex
657	  % $$
658	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
659	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
660	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
661	  % \ldots
662	  % $$"
663	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
664	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
665	  complex of localisations).}
666	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
667	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
668	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
669	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
670	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
671	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
672	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
673	  \[
674	    D^\bullet : \quad
675	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
676	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
677	    \cdots,
678	    \qquad
679	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
680	  \]
681	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
682	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
683	  \[
684	    1 \leq p
685	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
686	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
687	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
688	  \]
689	  which is the form the affine vanishing
690	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
691	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
692	  homomorphisms,
693	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
694	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
695	
696	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
697	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
698	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
699	  product}
700	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
701	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
702	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
703	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
704	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
705	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
706	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
707	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
708	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
709	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
710	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
711	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
712	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
713	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
714	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
715	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
716	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
717	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
718	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
719	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
720	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
721	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
722	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
723	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
724	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
725	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
726	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
727	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
728	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
729	\end{lemma}
730	
731	\begin{proof}
732	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
733	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
734	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
735	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
736	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
737	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
738	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
739	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
740	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
741	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
742	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
743	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
744	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
745	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
746	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
747	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
748	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
749	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
750	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
751	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
752	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
753	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
754	  element-level product equivalence
755	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
756	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
757	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
758	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
759	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
760	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
761	
762	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
763	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
764	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
765	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
766	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
767	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
768	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
769	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
770	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
771	  being proved.
772	\end{proof}

830	  \(M\). Assembling the coordinatewise isomorphisms after the product equivalence gives

852	  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}
58

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}

92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}

126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair,
273	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ₁, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.f_comp_horseshoeβ₁,
274	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.g_comp_horseshoeH,
275	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero,
276	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero_hf, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_zero,
277	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_fst,
278	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_snd, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0,
279	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_τZero,
280	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero}
281	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
282	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
283	  \[
284	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
285	  \]
286	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
287	  identity
288	  \[
289	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
290	  \]
291	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
292	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
293	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
294	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
295	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
296	\end{lemma}
297	
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
300	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
301	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
302	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
303	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
304	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
305	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
306	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
307	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
308	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
309	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
310	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
311	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
313	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
314	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
315	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
316	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
317	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
318	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
319	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
320	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
322	\end{proof}

324	\begin{lemma}\leanok
325	[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
326	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
327	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_comp}
328	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
329	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
330	  differential degreewise by the matrix
331	  \[
332	    d_B^n \;=\;
333	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
334	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
335	  \]
336	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
337	  cochain complex.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
342	  \leanok
343	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
344	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
345	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
346	  \[
347	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
348	  \]
349	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
350	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
351	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
352	\end{proof}

355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,

362	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd}

365	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
366	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
367	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that

390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
391	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
392	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
393	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
394	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}

433	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,

435	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
436	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).

443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
445	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes
450	  \[
451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]
454	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
458	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
459	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
460	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
461	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
462	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
463	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
464	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
465	\end{proof}

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}
