515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
700	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
701	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).

716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment

740	  \]

742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):

900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).

909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)

917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).

1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.

1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).

1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the

1040	  whose inverse-direction coordinate is \(\operatorname{sectionProdEquiv\_symm\_apply}\). The two commuting squares of the ladder are the
1041	  coface match: the abstract unfold of the differential as an alternating sum of face
1042	  restrictions (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}, the decl
1043	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_objD\_apply}\)) together with the per-coordinate
1044	  identification of each face restriction with the localisation coface
1045	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}) shows
1046	  \(e_{p+1} \circ \operatorname{objD} p = \operatorname{dDiff}^{\,p} \circ e_p\). The
1047	  horizontal exactness fed into the transport is the \(R\)-module exactness
1048	  \(H = \operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
1049	  Transporting \(H\) across the additive ladder yields the desired
1050	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the \(\operatorname{objD}\) maps, discharging the
1051	  hypothesis of the precursor.
1052	\end{proof}

1081	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
1082	  the global sections of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
1083	  R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) a spanning
1084	  family of the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) =
1085	  \top\) (Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}). The \emph{un-localised section
1086	  {\v C}ech module complex} \(D^\bullet\) is the cochain complex of \(R\)-modules
1087	  whose degree-\(p\) term is the product over multi-indices
1088	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of the away localisations
1089	  \[
1090	    D^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} M_{s_\sigma},
1091	    \qquad s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
1092	    \qquad M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}
1093	      \bigl(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\bigr)\, M,
1094	  \]
1095	  with differential \(d^p : D^p \to D^{p+1}\) the alternating sum
1096	  \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j\, \delta^j\) of the coface comparison maps \(\delta^j\), each of
1097	  which is the canonical away-localisation comparison
1098	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) sending a fraction to its image under
1099	  the further localisation at the omitted factor. The single-coefficient comparison
1100	  map and its localisation property are recorded by the \(\mathtt{dCoface}\) and
1101	  \(\mathtt{dCoeff}\) data and the differential by \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) (with action
1102	  \(\mathtt{dDiff\_apply}\)); the auxiliary \(\mathtt{spanIdx}\)/\(\mathtt{spanIdx\_spec}\)
1103	  select a witnessing index of the spanning relation.
1104	
1105	  The complex \(D^\bullet\) is tied to the section {\v C}ech complex of
1106	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three
1107	  comparison families. First, the maps \(\mathtt{dToCech}\) identify each term
1108	  \(D^p\) with the degree-\(p\) section group
1109	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) of
1110	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
1111	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
1112	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side
1113	  cofaces are \(\mathtt{cechCofaceLin}\) with action \(\mathtt{cechCoface\_apply}\).
1114	  Second, localising \(D^\bullet\) at a spanning element \(s_r\) reproduces the
1115	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1116	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
1117	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial
1118	  differential, and its exactness is recorded as \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\). The
1119	  localisation comparison maps \(\mathtt{fLoc}\) (action \(\mathtt{fLoc\_apply}\))
1120	  exhibit each localised term as a localised module
1121	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
1122	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).
1123	  The key algebraic fact underlying all three families is the localisation
1124	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\): localising the away module \(M_{s_\sigma}\)
1125	  further at \(s_r\) gives \(M_{s_r s_\sigma}\). This is the keystone
1126	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\) (the composite of two away
1127	  localisations is again the away localisation at the product), with the cancellation
1128	  bookkeeping \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.Inverts.smul\_pow\_cancel}\). Thus localising
1129	  \(D^\bullet\) at \(s_r\) (an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.Away}\)) reproduces
1130	  the localised Čech complex of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, and
1131	  exactness of \(D^\bullet\) can be checked one localisation at a time.
1132	\end{definition}

1134	\begin{lemma}
1135	
1136	\leanok
1137	[Positive-degree exactness of the section {\v C}ech module complex]
1138	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact}
1140	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1141	  With the notation of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, the
1142	  un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\) is exact in positive
1143	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1144	  \[
1145	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(d^{p+1} : D^{p+1} \to D^{p+2},\;
1146	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).
1147	  \]
1148	  This is step~(a) of the affine vanishing: the node-by-node exactness of the
1149	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section
1150	  {\v C}ech complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, step~(c)) is read off.
1151	\end{lemma}
1152	
1153	\begin{proof}
1154	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1155	  \leanok
1156	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1157	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1158	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1159	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1160	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1161	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1162	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1163	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1164	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1165	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1166	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that
1167	  localised differential is \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\), which is in turn the
1168	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial exactness transported along
1169	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\); the contracting homotopy at the fixed index
1170	  \(r\) (where \(s_r\) is invertible) furnishes \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\),
1171	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the
1172	  spanning family yields the un-localised exactness.
1173	\end{proof}

1346	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1347	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1348	
1349	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1350	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1351	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1352	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1353	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1354	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1355	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1356	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1357	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1358	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1359	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1360	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1361	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1362	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1363	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1364	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1365	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1366	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1367	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1368	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1369	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1370

1394	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1395	  % \to
1396	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1397	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1398	  % $$
1399	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1400	  % and where the map
1401	  % $$
1402	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1403	  % \longrightarrow
1404	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1405	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}

1407	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1408	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1409	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1410	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1411	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1412	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1413	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1414	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1415	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1416	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1417	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1418	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1419	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1420	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1421	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1422	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1423	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1424	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1425	  \[
1426	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1427	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1428	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1429	  \]
1430	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1431	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1432	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1433	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1434	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1435	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1436	  \[
1437	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1438	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1439	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1440	  \]
1441	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1442	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1443	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1444	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1445	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1446	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1447	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1448	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1449	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1450	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1451	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1452	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1453	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1454	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1455	
1456	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1457	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1458	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1459	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1460	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1461	\end{definition}

1463	\begin{definition}
1464	
1465	[Section {\v C}ech complex of a presheaf of modules]
1466	  \label{def:section_cech_complex}

1468	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicial}

1470	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves,
1471	  % definition of $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
1472	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L879--910).
1473	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Set
1474	  % $$
1475	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1476	  % =
1477	  % \prod\nolimits_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p + 1}}
1478	  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1479	  % $$
1480	  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
1481	  % $$
1482	  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1483	  % \longrightarrow
1484	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p + 1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1485	  % $$
1486	  % by the formula
1487	  % $$
1488	  % d(s)_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}
1489	  % =
1490	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^{p + 1}
1491	  % (-1)^j
1492	  % s_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}|_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1493	  % $$
1494	  % It is straightforward to see that $d \circ d = 0$."
1495	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1496	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1497	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1498	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1499	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1500	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1501	  \[
1502	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1503	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1504	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1505	  \]
1506	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1507	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1508	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1509	  \[
1510	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1511	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1512	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1513	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1514	  \]
1515	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1516	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1517	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1518	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1519	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1520	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1521	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1522	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1523	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1524	\end{definition}

1570	% $$
1571	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(
1572	% (j_{i_0\ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_p}},
1573	% \mathcal{F})
1574	% =
1575	% \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1576	% $$

1578	% $$

1580	% (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}
1581	% $$
1582	% represents the functor
1583	% $$
1584	% \mathcal{F}
1585	% \longmapsto
1586	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0\ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1587	% $$
1588	% Hence by Categories, Yoneda Lemma \ref{categories-lemma-yoneda}

1592	\begin{proof}
1593	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1594	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1595	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1596	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1597	  \[
1598	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1599	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1600	    \bigr)
1601	    \;\cong\;
1602	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1603	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1604	    \;\cong\;
1605	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1606	  \]
1607	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1608	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1609	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1610	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1611	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1612	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1613	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1614	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1615	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1616	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1617	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1618	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1619	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1620	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1621	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1622	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1623	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1624	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1625	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1626	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1627	\end{proof}

1670	\begin{lemma}
1671	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1672	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1674	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1675	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1676	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1677	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1678	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1679	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1680	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1681	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1682	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1683	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1684	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1685	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1686	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1687	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1688	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1689	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1690	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1691	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1692	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1693	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1694	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1695	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1696	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1697	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1698	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1699	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1700	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1701	\end{lemma}
1702	
1703	\begin{proof}
1704	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1705	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1706	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1707	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1708	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1709	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1710	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1711	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1712	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1713	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1714	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1715	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1716	  quasi-isomorphism.
1717	\end{proof}

1719	\begin{lemma}
1720	
1721	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1722	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1723	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_X,
1724	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,
1725	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le}
1726	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1727	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1728	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1729	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1730	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1731	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1732	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1733	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1734	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1735	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1736	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1737	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1738	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1739	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1740	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1741	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1742	  \[
1743	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1744	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1745	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1746	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1747	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1748	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1749	  \]
1750	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1751	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1752	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1753	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1754	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1755	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1756	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1757	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1758	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1759	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1760	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1761	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1762	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1763	  The Lean entry point for the degree-\(p\) unfolding is
1764	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes
1765	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p = \coprod_\sigma \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)\) by
1766	  \(\operatorname{rfl}\)) composed with
1767	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\) at \(V\). The full degreewise
1768	  identification is assembled from three pieces: \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\)
1769	  (evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the coproduct, giving
1770	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_\sigma
1771	  (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\)),
1772	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\) (a surviving summand with
1773	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\)), and
1774	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\) (a summand with
1775	  \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) is the zero object); collapsing the coproduct over the
1776	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1777	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1778	\end{lemma}
1779	
1780	\begin{proof}
1781	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1782	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1783	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1784	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1785	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1786	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1787	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1788	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1789	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1790	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1791	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1792	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1793	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1794	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1795	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1796	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1797	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1798	\end{proof}

1839	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1840	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1841	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1842	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1843	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1844	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1845	  \[
1846	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1847	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, t_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}},
1848	  \]

1850	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1851	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),
1852	  \((\operatorname{combHomotopy}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, u)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1853	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1854	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)
1855	  (\(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_spec}\)), which yields positive-degree exactness
1856	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_exact}\) and the cocycle-splitting form
1857	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_eq\_of\_cocycle}\). The remaining declarations are
1858	  sign- and index-bookkeeping consumed by the contracting identity: the

1860	  alternating-sign flip \(\operatorname{combSign\_flip}\), together with the

1862	  category-theory helper \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) (a
1863	  coproduct of zero objects is a zero object) is recorded here as well, used to collapse

1868	  Stacks \texttt{lemma-homology-complex} proof.

2046	\begin{proof}
2047	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2048	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
2049	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
2050	  coproduct,
2051	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
2052	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
2053	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
2054	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
2055	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
2056	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
2057	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
2058	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
2059	  C_p\).
2060	
2061	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
2062	  applied to the alternating face sum
2063	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
2064	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
2065	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
2066	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
2067	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
2068	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
2069	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
2070	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
2071	  identifies the evaluated differential with
2072	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
2073	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
2074	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
2075	
2076	  \emph{The three layers commuted past.} The degreewise iso is the \(3\)-fold
2077	  composite
2078	  \[
2079	    (\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p)
2080	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\;}
2081	      \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0}
2082	        (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))
2083	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\;}
2084	      \coprod_{V \leq U_\sigma} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
2085	      \xrightarrow{\;(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \ldots)^{-1}\;}
2086	      C_p,
2087	  \]
2088	  whose layers are: (1) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) = the comparison
2089	  \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\) expressing that evaluation at
2090	  \(V\) commutes with the coproduct \(\coprod\); (2) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2091	  which kills the summands with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) (these evaluate to zero objects
2092	  by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\)); (3)
2093	  \((\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \operatorname{survivingEquiv}\
2094	  (\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\, \ldots))^{-1}\), which reindexes the
2095	  surviving summands by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) of
2096	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} and identifies each evaluated summand with the
2097	  single coefficient \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) via \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\).
2098	
2099	  \emph{Naturality past each layer.} The reindex \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) is
2100	  natural in the face map \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\):
2101	  a surviving index \(\sigma\) (i.e.\ \(V \leq U_\sigma\), equivalently
2102	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma\) by
2103	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\)) is carried to a surviving
2104	  \(\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (still \(V \leq U_{\sigma \circ
2105	  \operatorname{succAbove}\, i}\), since dropping a coordinate only weakens the meet),
2106	  so the drop-zeros layer (2) and the whisker layer (3) commute with every face. On a
2107	  surviving summand the evaluated transition \(\operatorname{freeYoneda}.\mathrm{map}
2108	  (\operatorname{homOfLE}\, \ldots)\) collapses to the identity under
2109	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); hence each face \(\delta_i\) matches the
2110	  engine reindexing \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\)
2111	  summand-for-summand, and summing with the \(\operatorname{objD}\) signs \((-1)^i\)
2112	  identifies the evaluated alternating-face differential with
2113	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) (\(= \) the chain transpose of
2114	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\)).
2115	
2116	  \emph{The genuine difficulty: variance.} The free \emph{chain} differential
2117	  \emph{lowers} \(\mathrm{Fin}\)-arity (\(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ
2118	  \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\), faces), whereas
2119	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) \emph{raises} it (it inserts an
2120	  index, summing over \(i \in I_1\)). The two are reconciled because on the finite
2121	  biproduct \(\coprod = \prod\) the insertion/deletion adjunction makes the index-drop
2122	  chain differential the transpose of the index-raise cochain differential; this
2123	  transpose is precisely what \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) of
2124	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} packages, and verifying the commutation amounts to
2125	  the injectionwise check \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) outlined above.
2126	\end{proof}

2463	  sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion

2465	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2466	  \[
2467	    \operatorname{sheafification} \;\dashv\; \iota,
2468	    \qquad
2469	    \iota = \text{the inclusion } X.\mathrm{Modules} \hookrightarrow
2470	    X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}.
2471	  \]
2472	  The left adjoint sheafification is exact, in particular it preserves
2473	  monomorphisms.
2474	\end{lemma}

2609	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{U} : U =
2610	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be any open covering, and let \(\mathcal{I}\) be an
2611	  \emph{injective} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the {\v C}ech cohomology of

2613	  \[

2616	      \mathcal{I}(U) & p = 0, \\
2617	      0 & p > 0.
2618	    \end{cases}

2622	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "An injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is also injective as an
2623	% object in
2624	% the category $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ (for example since
2625	% sheafification is an exact left adjoint to the inclusion functor,
2626	% using Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-adjoint-preserve-injectives}).
2627	% Hence we can apply Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}
2628	% (or its proof) to see the result."
2629	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective presheaf of
2630	% $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2631	% In this case the functor $\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})$
2632	% is exact on $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$. By
2633	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} we have
2634	% $$
2635	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I})
2636	% =
2637	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}).
2638	% $$
2639	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-homology-complex} we have that $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ is
2640	% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
2641	% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
2642	% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
2643	% $i > 0$."
2644	\begin{proof}
2645	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_op_identification, lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso, lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2646	  The argument has two independent parts.
2647	
2648	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2649	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2650	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2651	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2652	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2653	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
2654	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
2655	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2656	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2657	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2658	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2659	
2660	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
2661	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
2662	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
2663	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
2664	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
2665	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2666	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
2667	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
2668	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
2669	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
2670	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
2671	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
2672	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
2673	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2674	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).
2675	
2676	  \emph{Categorical assembly.} Concretely the exactness is produced by the
2677	  opposite-transport bridge. The augmentation \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}
2678	  \mathcal{U}\) is a quasi-isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso});
2679	  taking its opposite via the \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.op}\) machinery and
2680	  applying \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{I}\) keeps it a
2681	  quasi-isomorphism, since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules
2682	  (Part 1) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2683	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\),
2684	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2685	  mapped-opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isos
2686	  \(\operatorname{homCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2687	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_op_identification}) and
2688	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2689	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2690	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2691	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the mapped image of the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target
2692	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\). The latter has vanishing homology in positive
2693	  degrees, so the section {\v C}ech complex does too. Hence
2694	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
2695	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
2696	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
2697	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
2698	\end{proof}

2788	\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object}
2789	
2790	The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
2791	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
2792	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2793	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2794	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2795	\emph{Ext of a corepresenting object} in the single abelian category
2796	\(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\): there is an
2797	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresenting the
2798	sections-over-\(U\) functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and we set
2799	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2800	\mathcal{F})\). The corepresenting object is built \emph{without} the extension-by-zero
2801	functor — it is the sheafification of the free module on the representable presheaf
2802	\(\mathbf{y}\,U\) — and the realization keeps the injective module in the \emph{second}
2803	Ext argument, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf with \emph{no} appeal
2804	to restriction of module sheaves. The corepresenting object and its corepresentability
2805	are fixed first, then the following definition fixes the realization, and the Mathlib
2806	dependency anchors after it pin the exact results it rests on.
2807	
2808	\begin{definition}
2809	
2810	\leanok
2811	[Extension-by-zero structure sheaf as a corepresenting object]
2812	  \label{def:jshriek_ou}
2813	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU}
2814	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction,
2815	    lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2816	  \textit{Corepresenting object for sections over an open, built without the
2817	  extension-by-zero functor.}
2818	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subscheme, regarded through the
2819	  representable presheaf \(\mathbf{y}\,U\) on the site of opens of \(X\). The
2820	  \emph{extension-by-zero structure sheaf} \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) is the object of
2821	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) obtained by sheafifying the free
2822	  presheaf of modules on \(\mathbf{y}\,U\):
2823	  \[
2824	    j_!\mathcal{O}_U \;:=\; \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(
2825	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)\bigr)
2826	    \;\in\; X.\mathrm{Modules},
2827	  \]
2828	  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor on the
2829	  representable (the degree-zero generator of the free presheaf complex of
2830	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}) and
2831	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction of
2832	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}. Up to isomorphism this is the classical
2833	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) of the structure sheaf of \(U\) along the open
2834	  immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\); crucially, we build only this \emph{object} as a
2835	  sheafified free module, never the extension-by-zero \emph{functor} \(j_!\). The object
2836	  exists unconditionally: the free presheaf of modules on a representable and the
2837	  sheafification of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are both supplied by Mathlib.
2838	\end{definition}

2840	\begin{lemma}
2841	
2842	\leanok
2843	[Corepresentability of global sections over \(U\)]
2844	  \label{lem:jshriek_corepr}
2845	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv, AlgebraicGeometry.sheafificationHomAddEquiv}
2846	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2847	  \textit{The object \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the sections-over-\(U\) functor.}
2848	  For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) there is a natural additive
2849	  isomorphism
2850	  \[
2851	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
2852	    \;\cong\;
2853	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \;=\; \mathcal{F}(U),
2854	  \]
2855	  natural in \(\mathcal{F}\). Thus \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the global-sections
2856	  functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
2857	\end{lemma}
2858	
2859	\begin{proof}
2860	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2861	  \leanok
2862	  Compose two natural additive isomorphisms. The sheafification adjunction
2863	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) of
2864	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction} transports corepresentability through
2865	  sheafification:
2866	  \[
2867	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(
2868	      \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)),\, \mathcal{F}
2869	    \bigr)
2870	    \;\cong\;
2871	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2872	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr).
2873	  \]
2874	  The free–Yoneda additive equivalence \(\operatorname{freeYonedaHomAddEquiv}\) of
2875	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} then identifies the right-hand
2876	  presheaf Hom-group with the evaluation \((\iota\mathcal{F})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) =
2877	  \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\):
2878	  \[
2879	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2880	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr)
2881	    \;\cong\;
2882	    \mathcal{F}(U).
2883	  \]
2884	  Both isomorphisms are additive and natural in \(\mathcal{F}\), so their composite is
2885	  the asserted natural additive isomorphism.
2886	\end{proof}

2888	\begin{definition}
2889	
2890	\leanok
2891	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object]
2892	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2893	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology}
2894	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:jshriek_corepr, lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib,
2895	    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
2896	    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2897	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2898	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2899	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Let
2900	  \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \in X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be the corepresenting object of
2901	  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}. The \emph{absolute sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2902	  over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of this corepresenting object in the single
2903	  abelian category \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
2904	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2905	  \[
2906	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}
2907	      \bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}\bigr),
2908	    \qquad p \geq 0.
2909	  \]
2910	  Both Ext arguments live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); no restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2911	  along \(j\) is taken. Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2912	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); the
2913	  \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available unconditionally by
2914	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts the downstream
2915	  dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding Mathlib results:
2916	  \begin{enumerate}
2917	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
2918	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2919	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), by the
2920	    degree-zero Ext comparison (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); composing
2921	    with the corepresentability isomorphism
2922	    \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2923	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} gives \(H^0(U,
2924	    \mathcal{F}) \cong \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2925	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
2926	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\), one has
2927	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) by
2928	    Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Since \(\mathcal{I}\) is exactly
2929	    the second argument of \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I})\),
2930	    this yields \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly: the short exact sequence stays
2931	    in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), no restriction is taken, and
2932	    restriction-preserves-injectives is not needed.
2933	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
2934	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and the
2935	    \emph{fixed} first argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact
2936	    sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of
2937	    the groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext
2938	    class of the extension.
2939	  \end{enumerate}
2940	\end{definition}

2942	\begin{definition}
2943	[Ext bifunctor in an abelian category]
2944	  \label{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}
2945	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext}
2946	  \mathlibok
2947	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2948	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2949	  For an abelian category \(C\) with a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure and objects \(X, Y
2950	  \in C\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), Mathlib provides the Ext group
2951	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^n_C(X, Y)\), functorial (contravariantly in \(X\), covariantly in
2952	  \(Y\)) and equipped with a composition pairing.
2953	\end{definition}

3033	  isomorphism

3037	  \]

3044	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}) with the corepresentability isomorphism

3047	\end{proof}

3049	\begin{lemma}
3050	[Naturality of \(H^0 \cong \Gamma\) in the coefficient sheaf]
3051	  \label{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero_natural}
3052	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomologyZeroAddEquiv_naturality}
3053	  \uses{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero, lem:jshriek_corepr}
3054	  \textit{Project-bespoke obligation.}
3055	  The isomorphism \(H^0(U, -) \cong \Gamma(U, -)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero} is
3056	  natural in the coefficient sheaf: for a morphism \(g : \mathcal{F}_1 \to \mathcal{F}_2\) in
3057	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) the square
3058	  \[
3059	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3060	      H^0(U, \mathcal{F}_1) & \longrightarrow & H^0(U, \mathcal{F}_2)\\
3061	      \cong\downarrow & & \downarrow\cong\\
3062	      \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_1) & \xrightarrow{\;g_U\;} & \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_2)
3063	    \end{array}
3064	  \]
3065	  commutes, where the top arrow is \(H^0(U, g)\), the functorial action of \(g\) on
3066	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^0\) in the second variable, and the bottom arrow is the sections map
3067	  \(g_U = g(U)\).
3068	\end{lemma}
3069	\begin{proof}
3070	  \uses{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero, lem:jshriek_corepr}
3071	  Under the degree-zero Ext comparison, post-composition by the degree-zero Ext class of \(g\)
3072	  corresponds to post-composition by \(g\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!
3073	  \mathcal{O}_U, -)\); under the corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr}
3074	  (which is natural in the second variable, being a composite of the sheafification adjunction and
3075	  the free–Yoneda evaluation, both natural), this in turn corresponds to applying \(g\) on sections
3076	  over \(U\), i.e. \(g_U\). Hence the square commutes. In particular, when \(g_U\) is surjective so
3077	  is \(H^0(U, g)\) — the transfer used by the surjectivity step of
3078	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}.
3079	\end{proof}

3209	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
3210	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
3211	  % $$
3212	  % since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
3213	  % values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1)."
3214	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
3215	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
3216	  Let \(X\), \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\mathrm{Cov}\) be as in
3217	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, and let
3218	  \[
3219	    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0
3220	  \]
3221	  be a short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules in which \(\mathcal{F}\) has
3222	  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\) (condition (3)). Then for
3223	  every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) the induced sequence of section {\v C}ech
3224	  complexes
3225	  \[
3226	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
3227	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
3228	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
3229	  \]
3230	  is a short exact sequence of complexes of abelian groups.
3231	\end{lemma}
3232	
3233	\begin{proof}
3234	  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:ses_cech_h1}
3235	  Fix \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\). By condition (1)
3236	  every index tuple \(i_0 \ldots i_p\) has \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\), so
3237	  each {\v C}ech term is a product
3238	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, -) = \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
3239	  (-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) of section groups over basic opens. For each basic open
3240	  \(V = U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\), condition (2) supplies a cofinal system
3241	  of coverings of \(V\) lying in \(\mathrm{Cov}\), on which \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{F})\)
3242	  vanishes by condition (3); hence Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} makes the section
3243	  sequence
3244	  \(0 \to \mathcal{F}(V) \to \mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V) \to 0\) exact. (The only
3245	  nontrivial point is surjectivity of \(\mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\);
3246	  left-exactness \(0 \to \mathcal{F}(V) \to \mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\) is
3247	  automatic from left-exactness of sections on the short exact sequence of sheaves.) A
3248	  product of short exact sequences of abelian groups is short exact, so the sequence is
3249	  short exact in each degree \(p\). The {\v C}ech differentials are the alternating sums
3250	  of restriction maps, which are natural in the coefficient module, so the two maps are
3251	  chain maps; thus the displayed sequence is a short exact sequence of complexes.
3252	\end{proof}

3254	\begin{lemma}
3255	[Quotient preserves vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology]
3256	  \label{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}

3258	  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:cech_ses_of_basis, lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
3259	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
3260	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, long-exact-sequence step) (read from
3261	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
3262	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech
3263	  % cohomology groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
3264	  % This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
3265	  % with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$."
3266	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
3267	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
3268	  With notation as in Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}, assume moreover that
3269	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the quotient
3270	  \(\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\) again has vanishing higher {\v C}ech
3271	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\): \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for all
3272	  \(p > 0\) and every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\).
3273	\end{lemma}
3274	
3275	\begin{proof}
3276	  \uses{lem:cech_ses_of_basis, lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
3277	  Fix \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\). The short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
3278	  of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} yields a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech
3279	  cohomology
3280	  \[
3281	    \cdots \to \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
3282	      \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to
3283	      \check{H}^{p+1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to \cdots.
3284	  \]
3285	  For \(p > 0\) the left term \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) since
3286	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}), and the right
3287	  term \(\check{H}^{p+1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) by condition (3) for
3288	  \(\mathcal{F}\). Exactness forces \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\). As
3289	  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) was arbitrary, \(\mathcal{Q}\) has vanishing higher
3290	  {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\).
3291	\end{proof}

3321	\begin{proof}
3322	  \uses{def:absolute_cohomology, lem:cech_ses_of_basis,
3323	    lem:absolute_cohomology_covariant_les, lem:absolute_cohomology_injective_vanishing,
3324	    lem:absolute_cohomology_zero, lem:absolute_cohomology_zero_natural}
3325	  Run the covariant Ext long exact sequence
3326	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) of
3327	  \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) at the fixed first
3328	  argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), giving the exact strand
3329	  \[
3330	    H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \xrightarrow{\ g\ } H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})
3331	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \to H^1(U, \mathcal{I}).
3332	  \]
3333	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib} (the injective \(\mathcal{I}\) is
3334	  the \emph{second} Ext argument, Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_injective_vanishing})
3335	  the term \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) =
3336	  \operatorname{Ext}^1(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\). In degree \(0\) the
3337	  identification \(H^0(U, -) = \Gamma(U, -)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero}
3338	  is natural in the coefficient sheaf
3339	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero_natural}), so it carries \(g = H^0(U,
3340	  \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q})\) to the section map
3341	  \(\mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U)\); the latter is surjective by the section-level
3342	  exactness established inside Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} (applied at the basic
3343	  open \(U \in \mathcal{B}\)), hence so is \(g\). Surjectivity of \(g\) makes the connecting map
3344	  \(\delta = 0\); since also \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\), the strand reads
3345	  \(0 \to H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \to 0\), whence \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\).
